result:
The rain began without announcement, the kind that doesn't thunder but simply arrives, as if the sky had been holding its breath and finally let go. I was sitting by the window when it started, watching the light shift from gold to grey in the span of a breath. There is a particular silence that comes just before the rain reaches the ground—a hush, a waiting—and then the sound fills everything, not loud but omnipresent, like blood in the ears. It is a frequency that tunes out the higher pitches of anxiety, a natural white noise that resets something essential in the brain.

My coffee had gone cold. I had not noticed. The cup sat on the windowsill leaving a pale ring on the wood, and I thought about how everything leaves a mark, however faint. The wood will remember the moisture long after I wipe it away. The house will remember my weight on the floorboards, the particular creak of my step in the hallway at two in the morning when I cannot sleep. We are all making impressions on the world simply by existing, eroding it softly, being eroded in return. Even now, my fingerprints grease the glass; even now, my breath fogs a small circle that evaporates slowly, leaving nothing visible but taking something with it nonetheless.

I remember being seven years old and standing barefoot on our porch during a storm like this one. My mother had called me inside but I lingered at the threshold, where the overhang kept the dry concrete sacred while the yard beyond turned to dark soup. The boundary fascinated me—the exact line where safety became wildness. I put one toe across and felt the shocking cold of the water, then pulled back, then crossed again, playing at the edge of something I couldn't name. It was not defiance. It was curiosity about the moment of transition, the exact instant when dry became wet, when inside became out.

We spend so much of our lives in those thresholds without recognizing them. The moment before a name is spoken and a relationship changes forever. The second before the doctor speaks. The breath between sleeping and waking when you don't yet know who you are, where you are, whether the sorrow or joy you carry is real or dreamed. We think of life as a series of rooms with solid walls, but really it is all doorways, all liminal spaces where the temperature hasn't decided what it wants to be.

The rain is heavier now. It sheets down the glass, warping the view of the oak tree in the yard. The leaves have turned the silver-green they only become in downpours, underside-up, trembling. The tree is a hundred years old, maybe more. It has stood through wars I read about in books, through winters that cracked pavement, through the construction of this house and the one before it. It does not remember these events because a tree does not remember the way we do, yet it carries them in its rings, in the lean of its trunk, in the particular architecture of its branches where lightning struck once and the wood knuckled and grew around the wound. Memory without consciousness. History without grief. I have a book open on the table behind me, pages riffling in the draft, and I think of how paper was once pulp, once a tree like this one, breathing. Everything transforms; nothing is lost, only translated.

I wonder sometimes what the tree makes of me, if it makes anything at all. Perhaps I am simply a moving shadow behind glass, a temporary warmth, a flickering at night when I pace the kitchen. We are so brief, compared to oaks. And yet within that brevity we contain multitudes—every version of ourselves that ever was, layered like sediment. The child on the porch. The adolescent who believed pain was permanent. The adult learning that most things pass, even the ones we wish wouldn't. The self I was this morning, already obsolete, already becoming a story I will tell differently next year.

The neighbor's cat has taken shelter under my porch. I can see her through the gap in the boards, a grey comma curled around herself. She does not worry about the rain ending. She does not think about what she will do when it stops, does not make contingency plans or dwell on the inadequacy of her shelter. There is only the dry space, the sound above, the warmth of her own body, the steady rhythm of her breathing. I envy this. Not because I think ignorance is preferable, but because there is a kind of intelligence in acceptance that humans have largely abandoned in favor of constant prognostication. We are always elsewhere, preparing, regretting, anticipating. The cat is here. The cat is only here.

My phone buzzes on the table, a wasp against glass. I ignore it. The world will continue its spinning without my immediate attention; this is a lesson I am slowly, stubbornly learning. There is a tyranny to being constantly available, a subtle violence done to the present moment. The rain asks nothing of me. It does not need my opinion or my efficiency or my carefully worded opinions. It simply falls, and in doing so, grants me permission to simply be, to occupy space without justifying the occupation.

I think about all the people I have been. They do not disappear; they accumulate. I carry the child who was afraid of the dark, the young man who thought love was rescue, the person I was yesterday who believed a different set of worries would define the rest of his life. We are geological, really. Each year lays down a stratum. Sometimes there are earthquakes—loss, joy, trauma, grace—that shift the layers, fold them, bring what was buried to the surface dark and rich. We are never only who we are right now. We are the entire excavation, the whole canyon, the bedrock and the topsoil and the artifacts buried in between.

The coffee ring has dried on the windowsill, a pale ochre halo. I should clean it, but I don't. Let it stay as proof that I was here, that time passed, that the afternoon existed and was not entirely wasted on productivity. We are desperate for evidence. Photographs, scars, stains on wood, the worn spot on a book's spine. We know how quickly everything erodes, how the tide comes in regardless of what we've built, how even mountains become sand. The ring is a small resistance against that forgetting.

The rain will stop. The cat will leave, shaking each paw with precise disgust. I will pick up my phone and answer the buzzing and re-enter the stream of obligation and noise and the bright, flat light of screens. But for now, there is this: the grey light, the water on glass, the breathing of the house around me, the pages turning themselves. For now, I am sitting in the doorway between what was and what will be, one toe in the cold, one foot on the dry porch, fully alive in the threshold, waiting with the world.

I'll go with a quiet, reflective piece about a person sitting by a window on a rainy afternoon, letting thoughts drift through childhood, the nature of existence, small moments of beauty. The neighbor's cat has taken shelter under my porch. I can see her through the gap in the boards, a grey comma curled around herself. She does not worry about the rain ending. She does not think about what she will do when it stops. There is only the dry space, the sound above, the warmth of her own body. I envy this. Not because I think ignorance is preferable, but because there is a kind of intelligence in acceptance that humans have largely abandoned. We are always elsewhere. The cat is here.

My phone buzzes on the table. I ignore it. The world will continue its spinning without my immediate attention; this is a lesson I am slowly, stubbornly learning. There is a tyranny to being constantly available, a violence done to the present moment. The rain asks nothing of me. It does not need my opinion or my efficiency. It simply falls, and in doing so, grants me permission to simply be.

I think about all the people I have been. They do not disappear; they accumulate. I carry the child who was afraid of the dark, the young man who thought love was rescue, the person I was yesterday who believed a different set of worries. We are geological, really. Each year lays down a stratum. Sometimes there are earthquakes—loss, joy, trauma, grace—that shift the layers, fold them, bring what was buried to the surface. We are never only who we are right now. We are the entire excavation.

The coffee ring has dried on the windowsill, a pale ochre halo. I should clean it, but I don't. Let it stay as proof that I was here, that time passed, that the afternoon existed. We are desperate for evidence. Photographs, scars, stains on wood. We know how quickly everything erodes, how the tide comes in regardless of what we've built.

The rain will stop. The cat will leave. I will pick up my phone and answer the buzzing and re-enter the stream of obligation and noise. But for now, there is this: the grey light, the water on glass, the breathing of the house around me. For now, I am sitting in the doorway between what was and what will be, one toe in the cold, one foot on the dry porch, fully alive in the threshold.

The rain began without announcement, the kind that doesn't thunder but simply arrives, as if the sky had been holding its breath and finally let go. I was sitting by the window when it started, watching the light shift from gold to grey in the span of a breath. There is a particular silence that comes just before the rain reaches the ground—a hush, a waiting—and then the sound fills everything, not loud but omnipresent, like blood in the ears. It is a frequency that tunes out the higher pitches of anxiety, a natural white noise that resets something essential in the brain.

My coffee had gone cold. I had not noticed. The cup sat on the windowsill leaving a pale ring on the wood, and I thought about how everything leaves a mark, however faint. The wood will remember the moisture long after I wipe it away. The house will remember my weight on the floorboards, the particular creak of my step in the hallway at two in the morning when I cannot sleep. We are all making impressions on the world simply by existing, eroding it softly, being eroded in return. Even now, my fingerprints grease the glass; even now, my breath fogs a small circle that evaporates slowly, leaving nothing visible but taking something with it nonetheless.

I remember being seven years old and standing barefoot on our porch during a storm like this one. My mother had called me inside but I lingered at the threshold, where the overhang kept the dry concrete sacred while the yard beyond turned to dark soup. The boundary fascinated me—the exact line where safety became wildness. I put one toe across and felt the shocking cold of the water, then pulled back, then crossed again, playing at the edge of something I couldn't name. It was not defiance. It was curiosity about the moment of transition, the exact instant when dry became wet, when inside became out. I would stand there until my mother came and lifted me, her hands under my arms, and I would be surprised every time by the abruptness of being rescued from my own fascination.

We spend so much of our lives in those thresholds without recognizing them. The moment before a name is spoken and a relationship changes forever. The second before the doctor speaks. The breath between sleeping and waking when you don't yet know who you are, where you are, whether the sorrow or joy you carry is real or dreamed. We think of life as a series of rooms with solid walls, but really it is all doorways, all liminal spaces, all drafty in-between places where the temperature hasn't decided what it wants to be.

The rain is heavier now. It sheets down the glass, warping the view of the oak tree in the yard. The leaves have turned the silver-green they only become in downpours, underside-up, trembling. The tree is a hundred years old, maybe more. It has stood through wars I read about in books, through winters that cracked pavement, through the construction of this house and the one before it. It does not remember these events because a tree does not remember the way we do, yet it carries them in its rings, in the lean of its trunk, in the particular architecture of its branches where lightning struck once and the wood knuckled and grew around the wound. Memory without consciousness. History without grief. I have a book open on the table behind me, pages riffling in the draft, and I think of how paper was once pulp, once a tree like this one, breathing. Everything transforms; nothing is lost, only translated.

I wonder sometimes what the tree makes of me, if it makes anything at all. Perhaps I am simply a moving shadow behind glass, a temporary warmth, a flickering at night when I pace the kitchen. We are so brief, compared to oaks. And yet within that brevity we contain multitudes—every version of ourselves that ever was, layered like sediment. The child on the porch. The adolescent who believed pain was permanent. The adult learning that most things pass, even the ones we wish wouldn't. The self I was this morning, already obsolete, already becoming a story I will tell differently next year.

The neighbor's cat has taken shelter under my porch. I can see her through the gap in the boards, a grey comma curled around herself. She does not worry about the rain ending. She does not think about what she will do when it stops, does not make contingency plans or dwell on the inadequacy of her shelter. There is only the dry space, the sound above, the warmth of her own body, the steady rhythm of her breathing. I envy this. Not because I think ignorance is preferable, but because there is a kind of intelligence in acceptance that humans have largely abandoned in favor of constant prognostication. We are always elsewhere, preparing, regretting, anticipating. The cat is here. The cat is only here.

My phone buzzes on the table, a wasp against glass. I ignore it. The world will continue its spinning without my immediate attention; this is a lesson I am slowly, stubbornly learning. There is a tyranny to being constantly available, a subtle violence done to the present moment. The rain asks nothing of me. It does not need my opinion or my efficiency or my carefully worded opinions. It simply falls, and in doing so, grants me permission to simply be, to occupy space without justifying the occupation.

I think about all the people I have been. They do not disappear; they accumulate. I carry the child who was afraid of the dark, the young man who thought love was rescue, the person I was yesterday who believed a different set of worries would define the rest of his life. We are geological, really. Each year lays down a stratum. Sometimes there are earthquakes—loss, joy, trauma, grace—that shift the layers, fold them, bring what was buried to the surface dark and rich. We are never only who we are right now. We are the entire excavation, the whole canyon, the bedrock and the topsoil and the artifacts buried in between.

The coffee ring has dried on the windowsill, a pale ochre halo. I should clean it, but I don't. Let it stay as proof that I was here, that time passed, that the afternoon existed and was not entirely wasted on productivity. We are desperate for evidence. Photographs, scars, stains on wood, the worn spot on a book's spine. We know how quickly everything erodes, how the tide comes in regardless of what we've built, how even mountains become sand. The ring is a small resistance against that forgetting.

The rain will stop. The cat will leave, shaking each paw with precise disgust. I will pick up my phone and answer the buzzing and re-enter the stream of obligation and noise and the bright, flat light of screens. But for now, there is this: the grey light, the water on glass, the breathing of the house around me, thePages turning themselves. For now, I am sitting in the doorway between what was and what will be, one toe in the cold, one foot on the dry porch, fully alive in the threshold, waiting with the world.
